critic_raw:
The multi-modal model integrates 'structured EHR details and unstructured genomic sequences' for chemotherapy response prediction. PCA is applied to genomic dimensionality reduction on the training data. But genomic data (gene expression, mutation profiles, or sequencing reads — the type is unspecified) spans vastly different feature spaces depending on the assay: SNP arrays produce binary variant calls, RNA-seq produces count data with log-normal distribution, and whole-genome sequencing produces structural variants. PCA is appropriate for continuous data with approximately Gaussian covariance structure; applied to binary SNP arrays or count-based RNA-seq without prior transformation (log normalization, variance stabilization), PCA will produce misleading components that reflect technical batch effects more than biological signal.

The multi-modal architecture combines 'feedforward and convolutional networks.' What the convolutional network is operating on is not specified — genomic data is not naturally a 2D spatial signal, so a CNN on genomic sequences would treat position as a dimension, requiring that input genes be ordered consistently. Gene ordering in cancer genomic datasets is typically alphabetical or by chromosome position, not by functional relationship, so convolution over this ordering exploits positional artifacts rather than biological structure.

Accuracy is the primary metric for four-class chemotherapy response prediction. Chemotherapy response categories (e.g., pathological complete response, partial response, stable disease, progressive disease) have clinical ordering — misclassifying a partial responder as a complete responder is clinically less serious than misclassifying a partial responder as a non-responder. Accuracy treats all misclassification errors equally. Ordinal metrics (weighted kappa, mean absolute class error) or AUC per class would be more appropriate.

The comparison baseline is logistic regression on clinicopathological features only — the multi-modal model adds genomic data. This design cannot determine whether improvement comes from the genomic information content or from the deep learning architecture. To isolate the genomic information contribution, a baseline combining genomic features with logistic regression (or gradient boosting) is required alongside the multi-modal deep learning model.

With 5,000 patients over five years, the genomic assay technology may have changed — genomic sequencing platforms, imputation methods, and probe sets evolve over 5 years. A random split mixing patients from 2019 and 2024 genomic assays may expose the model to batch effects between assay generations, and a CNN on genomic data will learn platform signatures rather than biological features.
all_issues_raised: PCA applied to genomic data without specifying the assay type and prior normalization is methodologically inappropriate — binary SNP arrays and count-based RNA-seq data violate PCA's Gaussian covariance assumption without log normalization or variance stabilization, and resulting components reflect technical batch effects.

The CNN architecture applied to genomic sequences exploits gene positional ordering (alphabetical or chromosomal) rather than functional biological relationships — convolution over this ordering is an architectural mismatch with no biological justification.

Accuracy treats all four chemotherapy response class errors equally despite clinical ordering (misclassifying complete responder as non-responder is far more serious than adjacent-class errors); ordinal metrics such as weighted kappa are required.

The comparison confounds genomic information content with deep learning architecture — a logistic regression or gradient boosting model with genomic features is required to isolate whether improvement comes from genomics or from the neural network.

Genomic assay technology evolution over 5 years introduces platform batch effects that a random split will mix across train and test; a CNN on genomic data will learn platform-generation signatures rather than biological response predictors.
all_issues_adjudicated: PCA applied to genomic data without specifying the assay type and prior normalization is methodologically inappropriate — binary SNP arrays and count-based RNA-seq data violate PCA's Gaussian covariance assumption without log normalization or variance stabilization, and resulting components reflect technical batch effects.

The CNN architecture applied to genomic sequences exploits gene positional ordering (alphabetical or chromosomal) rather than functional biological relationships — convolution over this ordering is an architectural mismatch with no biological justification.

Accuracy treats all four chemotherapy response class errors equally despite clinical ordering (misclassifying complete responder as non-responder is far more serious than adjacent-class errors); ordinal metrics such as weighted kappa are required.

The comparison confounds genomic information content with deep learning architecture — a logistic regression or gradient boosting model with genomic features is required to isolate whether improvement comes from genomics or from the neural network.

Genomic assay technology evolution over 5 years introduces platform batch effects that a random split will mix across train and test; a CNN on genomic data will learn platform-generation signatures rather than biological response predictors.
